strategy: single chunk (file too short to split)

pages.text:
2

Posterior pelvic tilt (PPT), more informally known as butt wink or pelvic tucking, is one of
the most common technique problems during deep squats. PPT greatly increases
shearing stress on the spine and puts the sacroiliac joint in a very injurious position.

Here are a series of examples of good and bad lumbo-pelvic posture during the squat.
Photos without a caption illustrate good technique.

Left: bad (flat spine, pelvis tucked under). Right: good (the lumbopelvic complex
remains in neutral alignment). Note that this is still slightly above parallel.

5

This may be considered excessive anterior pelvic tilt, but his depth's good. Like most
lifters, he can front squat deeper than he can back squat.

7

Though difficult to see from this angle, this looks like a genuine full squat with good
technique.

10

A deep front squat with great forward lean. Her lumbopelvic alignment is still
acceptable, though she has almost no arch in her lower back anymore. Pushing her
knees out further would most likely perfect her technique.

12

Extreme forward lean during the back squat with good lumbopelvic alignment.

15

Techniques cues to prevent PPT
1) Push your knees out further. Your knees should stay aligned roughly over your toes.
Many people need to push their knees out further than they do intuitively to achieve
this, especially if their feet are pointed outward 45° or more.
2) Think 'ass up' when squatting. You do not need to arch your back or anteriorly tilt
your pelvis past anatomical position, but many people need to think this just to keep
a neutral lumbopelvic alignment.
3) Think of the squat as 'sitting down in between your legs' rather than 'folding over'.
4) Use the Valsalva maneuver: breathe in deeply through your abdomen before
descending into the squat and keep the air inside until you're past the sticking point
on the way up. When done properly, your belly swells up as if you just became
several months pregnant. This creates intra-abdominal pressure, which improves
force transfer and helps you lift a bit more weight.
5) Descend slowly and maybe even pause briefly in the bottom position until your
technique is good enough to be more explosive. It's easier to focus and maintain
good technique when you move slowly.
6) Squat down with a broomstick kept against your back (see image below) or
someone's hand on your lower spine. This will provide tactile feedback when your
lumbopelvic complex changes position so you can recognize it more easily.

HOW TO PREVENT BUTTWINK


--- Page 2 ---

Posterior pelvic tilt (PPT), more informally known as butt wink or pelvic tucking, is one of
the most common technique problems during deep squats. PPT greatly increases
shearing stress on the spine and puts the sacroiliac joint in a very injurious position.

Here are a series of examples of good and bad lumbo-pelvic posture during the squat.
Photos without a caption illustrate good technique.

remains in neutral alignment). Note that this is still slightly above parallel.

Techniques cues to prevent PPT
1) Push your knees out further. Your knees should stay aligned roughly over your toes.
Many people need to push their knees out further than they do intuitively to achieve
this, especially if their feet are pointed outward 45° or more.
2) Think 'ass up' when squatting. You do not need to arch your back or anteriorly tilt
your pelvis past anatomical position, but many people need to think this just to keep
a neutral lumbopelvic alignment.
3) Think of the squat as 'sitting down in between your legs' rather than 'folding over'.
4) Use the Valsalva maneuver: breathe in deeply through your abdomen before
descending into the squat and keep the air inside until you're past the sticking point
on the way up. When done properly, your belly swells up as if you just became
several months pregnant. This creates intra-abdominal pressure, which improves
force transfer and helps you lift a bit more weight.
5) Descend slowly and maybe even pause briefly in the bottom position until your
technique is good enough to be more explosive. It's easier to focus and maintain

6) Squat down with a broomstick kept against your back (see image below) or
